author: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon
book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

lineage: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon.
The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

metadata.author: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon

title: Canon VI.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Neither presbyter, deacon, nor any of the ecclesiastical order shall be ordained at large, nor unless the person ordained is particularly appointed to a church in a city or village, or to a martyry, or to a monastery.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: And if any have been ordained without a charge, the holy Synod decrees, to the reproach of the ordainer, that such an ordination shall be inoperative, and that such shall nowhere be suffered to officiate.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Ancient Epitome of Canon VI.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: In Martyries and Monasteries ordinations are strictly forbidden.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Should any one be ordained therein, his ordination shall be reputed of no effect.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The wording of the canon seems to intimate that the synod of Chalcedon held ordinations of this sort to be not only illicit but also invalid, irritis and cassis.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Nor is this to be wondered at, if we take into account the pristine and ancient discipline of the church and the opinion of many of the Scholastics (Morinus, De SS.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: It is clear that our canon forbids the so-called absolute ordinations, and requires that every cleric must at the time of his ordination be designated to a definite church.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The only titulus which is here recognized is that which was later known as titulus beneficii.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: As various kinds of this title we find here (a) the appointment to a church in the city; (b) to a village church; (c) that to the chapel of a martyr; (d) the appointment as chaplain of a monastery.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: For the right understanding of the last point, it must be remembered that the earliest monks were in no wise clerics, but that soon the custom was introduced in every larger convent, of having at least one monk ordained presbyter, that he might provide for divine service in the monastery.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Similar prohibitions of ordinationes absolutæ were also put forth in after times.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: According to existing law, absolute ordinations, as is well known, are still illicitæ, but yet validæ, and even the Council of Chalcedon has not declared them to be properly invalidæ, but only as without effect (by permanent suspension).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

verses.text: By the word μαρτυρίῳ (“martyry”) is meant a church or chapel raised over a martyr’s grave.

verses.text: So the Laodicene Council forbids Churchmen to visit the “martyries of heretics” (can. ix.).
